title: 3 Legal Considerations
body_text:
3	Legal Considerations
3.1	Overview of Legal Considerations
Credit unions are responsible for complying with applicable legislation and regulations. The information provided in this Chapter is for convenience only and doesnât take the place of statutes and regulations. Reference should be made to the actual statute or regulation for the full text. Note that there is no guarantee of accuracy for the online statutes.
Note:	Other federal and provincial legislation may influence Canadian dollar Money Orders (e.g. privacy legislation; human rights legislation). To address this issue, the credit union must apply additional policies and procedures.
3.2	CPA Rules
The Canadian Payments Association (CPA) develops and implements the rules that apply to the clearing and settlement of different types of payments between its member financial institutions. Refer to the CPA website for more information on CPA Rules.
CPA Rule A1, General Rules Pertaining to Items Acceptable for Exchange for the Purpose of Clearing and Settlement
â This Rule specifies that Money Orders are payment items that are acceptable for exchange and settlement through the Clearing.
CPA Rule A4, Returned and Redirected Items
â This Rule states that Money Orders canât be returned for the reason NSF, stale dated, funds not cleared, or payment stopped.
3.3	Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act and Regulations (Canada)
The
Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act (PCMLTFA)
and
PCMLTF Regulations
apply to financial institutions and their agents (e.g., deposit-taking agents). Contact your AML Compliance Officer or FINTRAC for more information. Subscribers to the Operations Manual Program can also refer to the PCMLTFA Compliance Part.
END
